summary: In this session, student_c focused on exam preparation, specifically regarding the scoring for various sections, including Evidence naming (10%), Fault analysis (15%), and Trace reasoning (25%). The user sought clarification on the distinction between thin and fabricated evidence, learning that thin evidence can still support a valid claim, while fabricated evidence is misleading. The assistant emphasized the importance of understanding consensus protocols, recommending a focus on Paxos and Raft due to their frequent examination. Student_c also inquired about vector clocks, confirming they are worth 5 marks, and acknowledged their lesser emphasis in the exam. The user expressed a desire to practice with past exams to boost confidence and solidify their understanding of the material. The assistant advised effective time management during the exam and starting with questions the user feels most confident about. Overall, student_c left the session feeling prepared and with a solid study plan, reinforcing their strategies for high-yield topics while remaining open to reviewing less emphasized areas.
key_facts: Evidence naming is worth 10% of the evaluation.
Fault analysis is worth 15% and requires clear distinctions between thin and fabricated evidence.
Trace reasoning carries 25% of the evaluation and requires correct reading of relations.
Vector clocks are worth 5 marks but are less emphasized in the exam.
positions_taken: Student_c plans to focus on practicing with past exams.
The user intends to manage their time effectively during the exam.
